request.question:
Applying a chitosan hydrogel scaffold loaded with 10 ng/mL epidermal growth factor to full-thickness dermal wounds in Sprague-Dawley rats will accelerate wound closure by at least 35% at day 7 compared to untreated controls, due to sustained EGF release promoting keratinocyte migration.
In plain English: does a growth-factor-loaded gel help wounds close faster?

hypothesis.intervention: chitosan hydrogel scaffold loaded with 10 ng/mL epidermal growth factor
hypothesis.biological_system: full-thickness dermal wounds in Sprague-Dawley rats
hypothesis.threshold: at least 35% at day 7 compared to untreated controls
hypothesis.mechanistic_rationale: due to sustained EGF release promoting keratinocyte migration
hypothesis.readiness_rationale: All core fields are present and specific. CRO-ready today.
hypothesis.literature_search_hint: chitosan hydrogel epidermal growth factor Sprague-Dawley rats wound closure
hypothesis.original_hypothesis:
Applying a chitosan hydrogel scaffold loaded with 10 ng/mL epidermal growth factor to full-thickness dermal wounds in Sprague-Dawley rats will accelerate wound closure by at least 35% at day 7 compared to untreated controls, due to sustained EGF release promoting keratinocyte migration.

references.title: Evaluation of chitosan gel containing liposome‐loaded epidermal growth factor on burn wound healing
references.relevance_note: This paper evaluates a chitosan gel with epidermal growth factor specifically on burn wound healing, which aligns with the intervention focus of the hypothesis.

references.title: Novel silver and nanoparticle-encapsulated growth factor co-loaded chitosan composite hydrogel with sustained antimicrobility and promoted biological properties for diabetic wound healing.
references.relevance_note: This paper discusses composite hydrogels involving chitosan aimed at enhancing wound healing, relevant to the overall biological system and methodology.

references.title: Hybrid Freeze-Dried Dressings Composed of Epidermal Growth Factor and Recombinant Human-Like Collagen Enhance Cutaneous Wound Healing in Rats
references.relevance_note: This paper investigates the effects of epidermal growth factor in enhancing wound healing, which is central to the proposed hypothesis in terms of the intervention.

literature_qc.explanation: In the intervention dimension, the use of chitosan with epidermal growth factor is related, specifically seen in paper [2], which evaluates a chitosan gel containing liposome-loaded epidermal growth factor. In terms of the biological system, while the exact model differs, the focus on wound healing studies is present across various papers. There is a significant overlap in concepts, such as tissue engineering for wound repair that also appears in papers [1] and [3]. Also, although the exact experimental design differs, the overall experiment type is shared by the retrieved literature focused on wound healing. Thus, this suggests the existence of meaningful prior art.
literature_qc.recommended_action: Related publications exist. Review the references, cite relevant prior work in your plan, and explicitly state how your experiment differs or extends the existing approach.

plan.objective: To evaluate the effect of chitosan hydrogel scaffold loaded with 10 ng/mL epidermal growth factor on wound closure in full-thickness dermal wounds in Sprague-Dawley rats.
plan.experimental_design: This wound healing study will compare wound closure between Sprague-Dawley rats treated with chitosan hydrogel scaffold loaded with epidermal growth factor and untreated control rats. The study will assess wound closure percentage at day 7 post-treatment, determining the efficacy of the treatment.
controls: Chitosan hydrogel scaffold loaded with 10 ng/mL epidermal growth factor.
Untreated full-thickness dermal wounds in Sprague-Dawley rats.

step_by_step_protocol.description:
DAY 0 (PREP) | 1 h | Wound Preparation
Prepare full-thickness dermal wounds by excising a 1.5 cm × 1.5 cm section of skin from the dorsal side of each Sprague-Dawley rat under isoflurane anesthesia (2-3% v/v).
Expected outcome: Complete excision of skin without injury to underlying structures.

DAY 0 (PREP) | 1 h | Hydrogel Preparation
Dissolve 1 g of chitosan in 100 mL of 1% acetic acid solution at room temperature with stirring for 1 h to form a chitosan solution.
Expected outcome: Homogeneous chitosan solution without visible particles.

DAY 0 (PREP) | 30 min | EGF Loading
Add 10 ng/mL of epidermal growth factor to the chitosan solution, mix gently for another 30 min to ensure complete incorporation of EGF.
Expected outcome: EGF is uniformly mixed within the chitosan solution.

DAY 0 (PREP) | 2 h | Gel Formation
Pour the chitosan-EGF solution into a mold of 1.5 cm × 1.5 cm and allow it to gel at room temperature for 2 h.
Expected outcome: Formation of chitosan hydrogel scaffolds.

DAY 0 (PREP) | 30 min | Application of Scaffold
After gel formation, gently place the chitosan hydrogel scaffold on the prepared wound area of the treatment group rats.
Expected outcome: Scaffolds are securely placed on the wounds.

DAY 1 | 30 min | Post-Operative Care
Monitor and provide analgesia (e.g., buprenorphine 0.05 mg/kg subcutaneously) to all rats once post-surgery to enhance recovery.
Expected outcome: Rats show signs of comfort post-surgery with stable vitals.

DAY 7 | 1 h | Wound Assessment
Measure the wound area using digital calipers to determine percentage closure compared to baseline measurements.
Expected outcome: Quantification of wound area with accuracy ±0.1 mm.

DAY 7 | 2 h | Data Analysis
Statistically analyze wound closure data using ANOVA followed by Tukey’s post-hoc test for multiple comparisons.
Expected outcome: Identification of significant differences in wound closure percentage between treatment and control groups.
Mitigation step for RISK-002: Add an explicit process control arm and a pre-run calibration checkpoint to the protocol.

assumptions: The chitosan hydrogel scaffold provides a conducive environment for wound healing.
EGF released from the chitosan hydrogel scaffold promotes keratinocyte migration and wound closure.
Sprague-Dawley rats will exhibit similar wound healing characteristics as reported in the literature.
decision_criteria: Proceed if wound closure in treated rats is ≥35% compared to untreated controls by day 7.
Stop the experiment if more than 20% of wounds show infection signs within the treatment period.
Re-evaluate the formulation if wound closure ≤10% in the treatment group after 7 days.
Pilot-only mode: do not scale to full run until replicate criteria are met.
risk_mitigations_applied: RISK-001: downgraded to pilot (Downgrade to pilot execution until at least three biological replicates confirm threshold performance.)
RISK-002: Add an explicit process control arm and a pre-run calibration checkpoint to the protocol.
reproducibility_notes: Ensure consistency in the wound size and model across all rats.
Standardize the chitosan solution preparation method for each experiment.

validation.primary_endpoint: Post-thaw viability percentage at immediate and 24h checkpoints.
validation.success_threshold: at least 35% at day 7 compared to untreated controls improvement over comparator in replicate-confirmed data.
failure_conditions: No meaningful viability gain in replicate-confirmed runs.
Intervention increases delayed viability loss at 24h.
validation.suggested_statistical_comparison: Two-group comparison against comparator using t-test or Mann-Whitney based on normality.
validation.minimum_replicates_or_design_note: Minimum three biological replicates before execution-level claim.

scope_of_work: Execute pilot-first HeLa cryopreservation comparison between DMSO baseline and trehalose arm.
Deliver replicate-confirmed viability analysis and mitigation traceability report.
